subject: Biology
unit: Unit 5: Human Health, Nutrition, and Disease

questions.subject: Biology
questions.unit: Unit 5: Human Health, Nutrition, and Disease
questions.question: What are diseases caused by pathogens such as bacteria, viruses, protozoa, or fungi called?
questions.hint: These diseases can be transmitted from person to person.
questions.explanation: Infectious diseases are caused by disease-causing organisms (pathogens) and can be transmitted from person to person. Option A, non-infectious diseases, are not caused by pathogens and cannot be transmitted.

source_trace.pdf_unit: Unit 5: Human Health, Nutrition, and Disease
source_trace.content_anchor: Infectious diseases are diseases that are caused by disease causing organisms. These types of diseases can be transmitted from person to person

explanations_tiered.definition: Infectious diseases are caused by pathogens (bacteria, viruses, protozoa, fungi) and can be transmitted between individuals.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Infectious diseases spread through direct contact, contaminated food/water, insect bites, or sexual intercourse, and include HIV, TB, malaria, and COVID-19.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Infectious diseases are sicknesses you can catch from someone else, like a cold or the flu, because tiny germs spread between people.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Infectious diseases result from infection by pathogens that multiply in the body and can be transmitted through various routes including droplets, blood, or vectors.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Pathogens such as Mycobacterium tuberculosis (bacterium), HIV (retrovirus), and Plasmodium (protozoan) cause infectious diseases by evading host immune defenses and spreading via specific transmission mechanisms.
reasoning_steps: Identify that the question asks for diseases caused by pathogens.
Recall that infectious diseases are caused by pathogens.
Recognize that non-infectious diseases have other causes.
Select infectious diseases.
misconceptions: Non-infectious diseases are sometimes confused with infectious ones, but they cannot be transmitted between people.
All infectious diseases are not caused by the same type of pathogen; different pathogens cause different diseases.

glossary_entry.definition: Diseases caused by pathogens such as bacteria, viruses, protozoa, or fungi that can be transmitted from person to person.

flashcard.back: Diseases caused by pathogens (bacteria, viruses, protozoa, fungi) that can be transmitted from person to person.
flashcard.glossary_definition: Diseases caused by pathogens such as bacteria, viruses, protozoa, or fungi that can be transmitted from person to person.
flashcard.teacher_note: Differentiate between infectious and non-infectious diseases with examples.
flashcard.solution_manual_note: Answer B. Infectious diseases are caused by pathogens and are transmissible; non-infectious diseases are not caused by pathogens.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: Pathogens such as bacteria, viruses, protozoa, and fungi cause infectious diseases, which can be transmitted between people.
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Diseases caused by pathogens such as bacteria, viruses, protozoa, or fungi that can be transmitted from person to person.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: List examples: HIV, TB, malaria, COVID-19, common cold.
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer B. Infectious diseases are caused by pathogens and are transmissible.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.unit: Unit 5: Human Health, Nutrition, and Disease
questions.question: A person drinks water contaminated with sewage containing disease-causing bacteria. Which mode of transmission is this?
questions.hint: The contamination came through drinking water.
questions.explanation: Drinking contaminated water or eating contaminated food is a mode of transmission for infectious diseases caused by pathogens that survive in water or food. Option A, direct contact, requires person-to-person touch, not water-borne transmission.

source_trace.pdf_unit: Unit 5: Human Health, Nutrition, and Disease
source_trace.content_anchor: Other infectious diseases are transmit by drinking contaminated water or by eating contaminated food, (food or water contains disease-causing organisms like bacteria and virus).

explanations_tiered.definition: Contaminated water or food is a mode of transmission where pathogens enter the body through ingestion.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Many infectious diseases, including cholera and giardia, are transmitted when people drink water or eat food contaminated with feces containing pathogens.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Drinking dirty water with germs is like swallowing tiny bugs that make you sick, which is how cholera and typhoid spread.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Fecal-oral transmission occurs when pathogens from sewage contaminate drinking water or food, causing gastrointestinal infectious diseases.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Waterborne pathogens such as Vibrio cholerae and Giardia lamblia are transmitted via the fecal-oral route, surviving in contaminated water until ingested by a new host.
reasoning_steps: Identify the source: contaminated water.
Recall that this is a specific transmission route.
Recognize that contaminated water/food is the correct mode.
Select contaminated water or food.
misconceptions: Direct contact requires person-to-person touch, not water-borne transmission.
Insect bites transmit malaria and dengue, not water-borne diseases.

glossary_entry.definition: A mode of infectious disease transmission where pathogens are ingested through contaminated drinking water or food.

flashcard.back: When people drink water or eat food contaminated with sewage containing disease-causing bacteria or viruses.
flashcard.glossary_definition: A mode of infectious disease transmission where pathogens are ingested through contaminated drinking water or food.
flashcard.teacher_note: Emphasize boiling drinking water and proper sanitation to prevent waterborne diseases.
flashcard.learning_objective_tag: Explain the modes of transmission of infectious diseases
flashcard.solution_manual_note: Answer D. Drinking contaminated water is transmission via contaminated water/food; direct contact is person-to-person touch; insect bite transmits malaria; sexual intercourse transmits STIs.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: Cholera is transmitted through drinking water or eating food contaminated with feces containing Vibrio cholerae bacteria.
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: A mode of infectious disease transmission where pathogens are ingested through contaminated drinking water or food.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Discuss importance of clean drinking water and sanitation.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Explain the modes of transmission of infectious diseases
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer D. Contaminated water/food is the mode; direct contact is person-to-person; insect bite is for malaria; sexual intercourse for STIs.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.unit: Unit 5: Human Health, Nutrition, and Disease
questions.sub_subsection: Human immune deficiency virus infection/ acquired immunodeficiency syndrome (HIV-AIDS)
questions.structural_anchor: Human immune deficiency virus infection/ acquired immunodeficiency syndrome (HIV-AIDS)
questions.question: A person infected with HIV gradually loses T-lymphocytes. Why does this lead to AIDS?

options.A: The virus directly damages all organs
options.B: Loss of T-lymphocytes reduces the body's disease resistance
options.D: The virus produces toxins that destroy bone marrow

questions.hint: T-lymphocytes are key cells in the immune system.
questions.explanation: HIV destroys T-lymphocytes, which defend the body against disease. As their number decreases, the immune system weakens, leading to AIDS and opportunistic infections. Option A is incorrect because HIV specifically targets immune cells, not all organs directly.

source_trace.pdf_unit: Unit 5: Human Health, Nutrition, and Disease
source_trace.structural_element: Human immune deficiency virus infection/ acquired immunodeficiency syndrome (HIV-AIDS)
source_trace.content_anchor: HIV replicates and survives in human T-lymphocyte cells. During its replication HIV, destroys these T-lymphocytes cells. As a result the number of T-lymphocytes gradually decrease which leads to declining of disease resistant mechanism of the body.

explanations_tiered.definition: HIV causes AIDS by destroying T-lymphocytes, weakening the immune system and allowing opportunistic infections.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: HIV infects and kills CD4+ T cells, progressively reducing immune function until the body can no longer fight off normally harmless pathogens.
explanations_tiered.beginner: HIV is like a thief that steals the body's soldiers (T-cells), so when other germs attack, there's no army to fight back.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: HIV targets CD4+ T-lymphocytes, using them to replicate and destroying them, leading to immunodeficieny and AIDS-defining opportunistic illnesses.
explanations_tiered.advanced: HIV integrates into host CD4+ T-cell genome, causing lytic replication and apoptosis; when CD4 count drops below 200 cells/μL, AIDS develops with susceptibility to Pneumocystis jirovecii, Mycobacterium avium, and Kaposi's sarcoma.
reasoning_steps: Identify that T-lymphocytes are immune cells.
Recall that HIV destroys these cells.
Recognize that fewer T-cells means less disease resistance.
Conclude that reduced resistance leads to AIDS.
misconceptions: Some think HIV directly causes death from the virus itself, but death results from opportunistic infections due to immune failure.
AIDS is not a single disease but a syndrome of opportunistic infections.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: HIV → destroys T-cells → weak immunity → AIDS.

glossary_entry.definition: Acquired Immunodeficiency Syndrome caused by HIV, resulting from destruction of T-lymphocytes and leading to opportunistic infections.

flashcard.front: How does HIV lead to AIDS?
flashcard.back: HIV destroys T-lymphocytes, weakening the immune system so the body cannot fight opportunistic infections.
flashcard.glossary_definition: Acquired Immunodeficiency Syndrome caused by HIV, resulting from destruction of T-lymphocytes and leading to opportunistic infections.
flashcard.teacher_note: Emphasize that AIDS is the late stage of HIV infection.
flashcard.learning_objective_tag: Explain the modes of transmission and prevention of HIV-AIDS
flashcard.solution_manual_note: Answer B. HIV destroys T-lymphocytes, reducing disease resistance and leading to AIDS; the virus does not directly damage all organs or produce toxins.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: Why does HIV infection lead to AIDS?
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: HIV destroys T-lymphocytes, progressively weakening the immune system until opportunistic infections develop.
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Acquired Immunodeficiency Syndrome caused by HIV, resulting from destruction of T-lymphocytes and leading to opportunistic infections.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Explain the difference between being HIV-positive and having AIDS.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Explain the modes of transmission and prevention of HIV-AIDS
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer B. HIV destroys T-lymphocytes, reducing immunity; virus does not directly damage all organs or produce toxins.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.unit: Unit 5: Human Health, Nutrition, and Disease
questions.question: A person with active tuberculosis coughs in a crowded bus. How are the TB bacteria most likely transmitted to others?
questions.hint: TB bacteria are released when an infected person coughs or sneezes.
questions.explanation: TB bacteria enter the air in tiny droplets when an infected person coughs or sneezes, and others inhale these droplets. Option A, contaminated water, is not the primary transmission route for TB.

source_trace.pdf_unit: Unit 5: Human Health, Nutrition, and Disease
source_trace.content_anchor: TB bacteria can enter the lungs in airborne droplets. It spread when infected people with the active form of the illness cough or sneeze. The bacteria are carried in the air in tiny droplets of liquid.

explanations_tiered.definition: Tuberculosis is transmitted through airborne droplets when an infected person coughs or sneezes.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Mycobacterium tuberculosis spreads via droplet nuclei that remain airborne, which can be inhaled by people in close or crowded spaces.
explanations_tiered.beginner: When someone with TB coughs, they spray tiny germs into the air like invisible dust, and others breathe them in.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: TB transmission occurs through inhalation of aerosolized droplet nuclei containing Mycobacterium tuberculosis, typically in crowded or poorly ventilated spaces.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Droplet nuclei (<5 μm) containing M. tuberculosis remain suspended in air for hours; inhalation allows bacilli to reach alveoli, where they are phagocytosed by macrophages but evade killing.
reasoning_steps: Identify the action: coughing in a crowded space.
Recall that TB spreads through the air.
Recognize that airborne droplets are the transmission mode.
Select airborne droplets.
misconceptions: TB is not spread through contaminated water; that is for cholera.
Insect bites transmit malaria, not TB.

glossary_entry.definition: A mode of infectious disease transmission where pathogens are carried in droplet nuclei and inhaled, as in tuberculosis.

flashcard.back: TB bacteria are spread through airborne droplets when an infected person coughs or sneezes; others inhale the bacteria.
flashcard.glossary_definition: A mode of infectious disease transmission where pathogens are carried in droplet nuclei and inhaled, as in tuberculosis.
flashcard.teacher_note: Discuss why crowded conditions and poor ventilation increase TB risk.
flashcard.learning_objective_tag: Explain the modes of transmission of infectious diseases
flashcard.solution_manual_note: Answer B. TB is transmitted through airborne droplets from coughs/sneezes; contaminated water transmits cholera; insect bites transmit malaria; sexual contact transmits STIs.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: Why is TB common in crowded places?
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: TB spreads through airborne droplets from coughs/sneezes; crowded, poorly ventilated spaces increase the chance of inhaling the bacteria.
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: A mode of infectious disease transmission where pathogens are carried in droplet nuclei and inhaled, as in tuberculosis.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Discuss BCG vaccine and treatment with multiple drugs.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Explain the modes of transmission of infectious diseases
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer B. TB is transmitted through airborne droplets; contaminated water is for cholera; insect bites for malaria; sexual contact for STIs.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.unit: Unit 5: Human Health, Nutrition, and Disease
questions.question: A person develops high fever and chills after being bitten by a mosquito. Which organism causes malaria and which mosquito transmits it?
questions.hint: Malaria is caused by a single-celled parasite, not a bacterium or virus.
questions.explanation: Malaria is caused by a protozoan called Plasmodium, transmitted by the bite of an infected female Anopheles mosquito. Option A is incorrect because bacteria do not cause malaria, and Culex mosquitoes transmit other diseases.

source_trace.pdf_unit: Unit 5: Human Health, Nutrition, and Disease
source_trace.content_anchor: Malaria is caused by protozoa known as plasmodium Protozoa. Female Anopheles mosquitoes feed on human blood... injects the plasmodium with her saliva.

explanations_tiered.definition: Malaria is caused by Plasmodium (a protozoan) and transmitted by female Anopheles mosquitoes.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: The malaria parasite has a complex life cycle involving both human and mosquito hosts, with transmission occurring when an infected mosquito takes a blood meal.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Malaria comes from a tiny animal called Plasmodium that lives in some mosquitoes, and when they bite you, the parasite gets into your blood.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Plasmodium is a protozoan parasite transmitted by Anopheles mosquitoes; sporozoites enter the human bloodstream during a blood meal and infect liver cells.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Plasmodium falciparum (among other species) undergoes sexual reproduction in Anopheles and asexual replication in human hepatocytes and erythrocytes, causing cyclic fever paroxysms.
reasoning_steps: Identify the cause: a parasite.
Recall that Plasmodium is a protozoan.
Recognize that Anopheles mosquito transmits it.
Select protozoan by Anopheles mosquito.
misconceptions: Some think malaria is caused by a virus or bacterium, but it is caused by a protozoan parasite.
Not all mosquitoes transmit malaria; only female Anopheles mosquitoes do.

glossary_entry.definition: An infectious disease caused by Plasmodium protozoa and transmitted by the bite of female Anopheles mosquitoes.

flashcard.front: What causes malaria and how is it transmitted?
flashcard.back: Malaria is caused by Plasmodium (a protozoan) and is transmitted by the bite of an infected female Anopheles mosquito.
flashcard.glossary_definition: An infectious disease caused by Plasmodium protozoa and transmitted by the bite of female Anopheles mosquitoes.
flashcard.teacher_note: Discuss malaria prevention: mosquito nets, insecticides, antimalarial drugs.
flashcard.learning_objective_tag: Explain the modes of transmission of infectious diseases
flashcard.solution_manual_note: Answer C. Malaria is caused by Plasmodium (protozoan) and transmitted by Anopheles mosquito; bacteria, viruses, and fungi do not cause malaria.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: Female Anopheles mosquitoes transmit malaria by injecting Plasmodium protozoa into the bloodstream when they bite.
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: An infectious disease caused by Plasmodium protozoa and transmitted by the bite of female Anopheles mosquitoes.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Explain the life cycle of Plasmodium (sexual in mosquito, asexual in human).
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Explain the modes of transmission of infectious diseases
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer C. Plasmodium (protozoan) causes malaria and is transmitted by Anopheles mosquito.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.unit: Unit 5: Human Health, Nutrition, and Disease
questions.question: A person with COVID-19 talks in a poorly ventilated room. How does the SARS-CoV-2 virus primarily spread to others?
questions.hint: The virus is released when an infected person breathes, talks, coughs, or sneezes.
questions.explanation: COVID-19 spreads primarily through respiratory droplets and aerosol particles released when an infected person breathes, talks, laughs, sings, coughs, or sneezes. Option A, contaminated food, is not the primary route for SARS-CoV-2.

source_trace.pdf_unit: Unit 5: Human Health, Nutrition, and Disease
source_trace.content_anchor: The COVID-19 virus spreads primarily through the droplets and virus particles released into the air when an infected person breathes, talks, laughs, sings coughs or sneezes.

explanations_tiered.definition: COVID-19 spreads primarily through respiratory droplets and aerosols from an infected person.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: SARS-CoV-2 is transmitted via close contact when respiratory particles containing the virus are inhaled or land on mucous membranes.
explanations_tiered.beginner: The COVID-19 virus spreads like invisible spray from your mouth when you talk, cough, or sneeze, and others breathe it in.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: SARS-CoV-2 transmission occurs through respiratory droplets (>5 μm) and aerosols (<5 μm) that remain airborne, especially in poorly ventilated indoor spaces.
explanations_tiered.advanced: SARS-CoV-2 uses ACE2 receptors for cell entry; viral RNA is detectable in aerosols for up to 3 hours, with superspreading events often linked to poor ventilation and prolonged exposure.
reasoning_steps: Identify the scenario: talking in a poorly ventilated room.
Recall that COVID-19 spreads through respiratory droplets.
Recognize that droplets and aerosols are the primary mode.
Select respiratory droplets and aerosols.
misconceptions: COVID-19 is not primarily spread through food or water; it is respiratory.
Insect bites do not transmit SARS-CoV-2.

glossary_entry.definition: An infectious disease caused by SARS-CoV-2, transmitted primarily through respiratory droplets and aerosols.

flashcard.back: SARS-CoV-2 spreads through respiratory droplets and aerosols released when an infected person breathes, talks, coughs, or sneezes.
flashcard.glossary_definition: An infectious disease caused by SARS-CoV-2, transmitted primarily through respiratory droplets and aerosols.
flashcard.teacher_note: Discuss prevention: masks, ventilation, distancing, hand washing, vaccines.
flashcard.learning_objective_tag: Explain the modes of transmission of COVID-19
flashcard.solution_manual_note: Answer B. COVID-19 spreads through respiratory droplets/aerosols; contaminated food and water are not primary routes; insect bites transmit other diseases.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: How can COVID-19 transmission be prevented?
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: Wearing masks, maintaining distance, improving ventilation, washing hands, and getting vaccinated reduce transmission of respiratory droplets.
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: An infectious disease caused by SARS-CoV-2, transmitted primarily through respiratory droplets and aerosols.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Explain the importance of ventilation and masks.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Explain the modes of transmission and prevention of COVID-19
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer B. COVID-19 spreads through respiratory droplets/aerosols; food/water and insects are not primary routes.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.unit: Unit 5: Human Health, Nutrition, and Disease
questions.question: A person touches a surface with the COVID-19 virus and then touches their mouth. What type of transmission does this represent?
questions.hint: The virus was on a surface (fomite), then transferred to the mouth.
questions.explanation: Fomite transmission occurs when a person touches a contaminated surface and then touches their mouth, nose, or eyes. Option A, airborne transmission, involves inhaling aerosols, not touching surfaces.

source_trace.pdf_unit: Unit 5: Human Health, Nutrition, and Disease
source_trace.content_anchor: It can also spread, if a person touches a surface with the virus on it and then touches his or her mouth, nose or eyes.

explanations_tiered.definition: Fomite transmission occurs when pathogens on surfaces are transferred to mucous membranes via touch.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: SARS-CoV-2 can survive on surfaces; touching contaminated surfaces and then the face can lead to infection, though this is less common than airborne spread.
explanations_tiered.beginner: If you touch a dirty doorknob and then rub your eyes, you can pick up germs from the surface and get sick.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Fomite transmission involves inanimate objects (fomites) contaminated with pathogens; the virus is transferred to a susceptible person through hand-to-face contact.
explanations_tiered.advanced: SARS-CoV-2 remains viable on plastic and stainless steel for up to 72 hours; fomite transmission risk depends on viral load, surface type, and time since contamination.
reasoning_steps: Identify the action: touching contaminated surface then mouth.
Recall this is called fomite transmission.
Recognize that fomites are contaminated objects.
Select fomite transmission.
misconceptions: Fomite transmission is different from airborne (inhalation) and droplet (direct spray) transmission.
Vector-borne transmission involves insects, not surfaces.

glossary_entry.definition: A mode of infectious disease transmission where pathogens on contaminated surfaces are transferred to mucous membranes by touch.

flashcard.back: When a person touches a contaminated surface and then touches their mouth, nose, or eyes, transferring the pathogen.
flashcard.glossary_definition: A mode of infectious disease transmission where pathogens on contaminated surfaces are transferred to mucous membranes by touch.
flashcard.teacher_note: Emphasize hand washing and surface disinfection to prevent fomite transmission.
flashcard.learning_objective_tag: Explain the modes of transmission of COVID-19
flashcard.solution_manual_note: Answer C. Fomite transmission occurs via contaminated surfaces; airborne/droplet are inhalation; vector-borne is via insects.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: How can touching surfaces lead to COVID-19?
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: If a person touches a surface with the virus and then touches their mouth, nose, or eyes, they can become infected (fomite transmission).
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: A mode of infectious disease transmission where pathogens on contaminated surfaces are transferred to mucous membranes by touch.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Hand washing and surface disinfection reduce fomite transmission.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Explain the modes of transmission of COVID-19
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer C. This is fomite transmission; airborne/droplet are via inhalation; vector-borne is via insects.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.unit: Unit 5: Human Health, Nutrition, and Disease
questions.sub_subsection: Preventing infections and control methods of infectious diseases
questions.structural_anchor: Preventing infections and control methods of infectious diseases
questions.question: Which method works by making the body defend itself against disease-causing organisms before infection occurs?
questions.hint: This method provides immunity without causing disease.
questions.explanation: Vaccination works by exposing the immune system to a harmless form of a pathogen, training the body to defend against future infections. Option B, antibiotics, treat bacterial infections after they occur, not prevent them.

source_trace.pdf_unit: Unit 5: Human Health, Nutrition, and Disease
source_trace.structural_element: Preventing infections and control methods of infectious diseases
source_trace.content_anchor: Vaccination: is a major control measure for many infectious diseases; it works by making the body to defend against disease causing organisms.

explanations_tiered.definition: Vaccination is a preventive method that trains the immune system to defend against specific pathogens.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Vaccines contain antigens that stimulate an immune response and memory cell production, providing long-term protection without causing disease.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Vaccination is like a practice drill for your immune system, teaching it how to fight a germ before the real germ attacks.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Vaccination introduces antigens from a killed or weakened pathogen, triggering antibody production and memory cells for rapid response upon later exposure.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Vaccines induce active acquired immunity by presenting epitopes to B and T cells, generating memory lymphocytes that provide rapid, specific protection upon subsequent infection.
reasoning_steps: Identify that the method works before infection.
Recall that vaccination prevents by training immunity.
Recognize that antibiotics treat after infection.
Select vaccination.
misconceptions: Antibiotics treat infections but do not prevent them; vaccination prevents.
Quarantine isolates infected individuals but does not create immunity.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Vaccination = train immune system before infection.

glossary_entry.definition: A method that makes the body defend against disease-causing organisms by stimulating immunity before infection.

flashcard.back: Vaccination exposes the immune system to harmless antigens, training it to produce antibodies and memory cells for rapid defense against future infection.
flashcard.glossary_definition: A method that makes the body defend against disease-causing organisms by stimulating immunity before infection.
flashcard.teacher_note: Discuss examples: BCG for TB, COVID-19 vaccines, polio vaccine.
flashcard.learning_objective_tag: Explain prevention and control methods of infectious diseases
flashcard.solution_manual_note: Answer A. Vaccination trains immunity before infection; antibiotics treat after infection; quarantine isolates; disinfection kills pathogens on surfaces.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: What is the difference between vaccination and antibiotics?
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: Vaccination prevents infection by training the immune system; antibiotics treat existing bacterial infections.
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: A method that makes the body defend against disease-causing organisms by stimulating immunity before infection.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Emphasize that vaccines are for prevention, not treatment.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Explain prevention and control methods of infectious diseases
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer A. Vaccination prevents by training immunity; antibiotics treat infection; quarantine isolates; disinfection cleans surfaces.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.unit: Unit 5: Human Health, Nutrition, and Disease
questions.question: Which condition is classified as a non-infectious disease?
questions.hint: This disease is caused by malnutrition, not by a pathogen.
questions.explanation: Kwashiorkor is a non-infectious disease caused by protein deficiency (malnutrition), not by a pathogen. Options A, C, and D are all infectious diseases caused by bacteria (TB), protozoa (malaria), or virus (COVID-19).

source_trace.pdf_unit: Unit 5: Human Health, Nutrition, and Disease
source_trace.content_anchor: These are disease caused by malnutrition, chemical effect, inherited or genetic factor etc. These diseases are not caused by disease causing organisms. Some of the non- infectious diseases... kwashiorkor, marasmus, scurvy

explanations_tiered.definition: Non-infectious diseases are not caused by pathogens and cannot be transmitted, including deficiency diseases like kwashiorkor.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Non-infectious diseases result from malnutrition, genetics, chemical exposure, or lifestyle factors, not from bacteria, viruses, or other pathogens.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Kwashiorkor is from not eating enough protein, not from catching germs like a cold, so you can't catch it from someone else.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Kwashiorkor is a protein deficiency disease (non-infectious), whereas tuberculosis, malaria, and COVID-19 are caused by infectious pathogens.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Non-infectious diseases have etiologies including nutritional deficiencies (kwashiorkor), genetic mutations (sickle cell anemia), and autoimmune processes (type 1 diabetes).
reasoning_steps: Identify that the question asks for a non-infectious disease.
Recall that kwashiorkor is caused by protein deficiency, not a pathogen.
Recognize that TB, malaria, and COVID-19 are infectious.
Select kwashiorkor.
misconceptions: Some think all diseases are contagious; non-infectious diseases like kwashiorkor cannot be caught from others.
Deficiency diseases are often misunderstood as infectious, but they result from nutritional imbalance.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Non-infectious = not caused by pathogens (e.g., kwashiorkor).

glossary_entry.definition: Diseases not caused by pathogens, resulting from malnutrition, genetics, chemical effects, or lifestyle factors.

flashcard.back: Kwashiorkor is non-infectious because it is caused by protein deficiency, not by a pathogen that can be transmitted.
flashcard.glossary_definition: Diseases not caused by pathogens, resulting from malnutrition, genetics, chemical effects, or lifestyle factors.
flashcard.teacher_note: Contrast infectious (HIV, TB, malaria) with non-infectious (kwashiorkor, marasmus, diabetes).
flashcard.solution_manual_note: Answer B. Kwashiorkor is a deficiency disease (non-infectious); TB, malaria, and COVID-19 are infectious diseases caused by pathogens.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: Non-infectious diseases are caused by malnutrition, genetics, chemical effects, or lifestyle factors, not by pathogens.
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Diseases not caused by pathogens, resulting from malnutrition, genetics, chemical effects, or lifestyle factors.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: List other non-infectious diseases: diabetes, cancer, anemia, rickets, scurvy, marasmus.
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer B. Kwashiorkor is non-infectious (protein deficiency); TB, malaria, COVID-19 are infectious.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.unit: Unit 5: Human Health, Nutrition, and Disease
questions.sub_subsection: Preventing infections and control methods of infectious diseases
questions.structural_anchor: Preventing infections and control methods of infectious diseases
questions.question: A community wants to prevent the spread of malaria. Which combination of methods directly targets the vector?
questions.hint: The vector for malaria is the Anopheles mosquito.
questions.explanation: Mosquito nets and insecticides directly target the Anopheles mosquito (vector), preventing bites and killing mosquitoes. Option A, vaccination and antibiotics, do not target the mosquito vector; there is no malaria vaccine widely available.

source_trace.pdf_unit: Unit 5: Human Health, Nutrition, and Disease
source_trace.content_anchor: Reducing the population of mosquitoes... using insecticide... avoiding mosquitoes bite by sleeping under a mosquito net

explanations_tiered.definition: Vector control methods for malaria include mosquito nets and insecticides that target Anopheles mosquitoes.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Malaria prevention focuses on reducing mosquito populations (insecticides) and preventing bites (mosquito nets), breaking the transmission cycle.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Mosquito nets create a barrier between you and the bugs, while insecticides kill the mosquitoes that carry malaria.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Insecticides reduce Anopheles mosquito populations, and insecticide-treated nets (ITNs) provide physical and chemical protection against bites.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Vector control strategies include indoor residual spraying (IRS) with pyrethroids, long-lasting insecticidal nets (LLINs), and larval source management to reduce malaria transmission intensity.
reasoning_steps: Identify that the vector is the Anopheles mosquito.
Recall methods that target mosquitoes directly.
Recognize that nets and insecticides target the vector.
Select mosquito nets and insecticides.
misconceptions: Vaccination and antibiotics do not target the mosquito vector; no malaria vaccine is widely available.
Boiling water and hand washing prevent waterborne diseases, not mosquito-borne malaria.

glossary_entry.definition: Methods to reduce or eliminate disease-transmitting vectors such as mosquitoes, including nets and insecticides.

flashcard.front: How can malaria be prevented by targeting the mosquito?
flashcard.back: Using mosquito nets to prevent bites and insecticides to kill mosquitoes directly targets the Anopheles vector.
flashcard.glossary_definition: Methods to reduce or eliminate disease-transmitting vectors such as mosquitoes, including nets and insecticides.
flashcard.teacher_note: Discuss other methods: eliminating standing water where mosquitoes breed.
flashcard.learning_objective_tag: Explain prevention and control methods of infectious diseases
flashcard.solution_manual_note: Answer C. Mosquito nets and insecticides target the mosquito vector; vaccination is for other diseases; boiling water for waterborne diseases; quarantine for isolating infected individuals.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: What are two methods of vector control for malaria?
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: Mosquito nets (to prevent bites) and insecticides (to kill mosquitoes) directly target the Anopheles vector.
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Methods to reduce or eliminate disease-transmitting vectors such as mosquitoes, including nets and insecticides.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Discuss other methods: larvicides, draining standing water, biological control (fish that eat mosquito larvae).
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Explain prevention and control methods of infectious diseases
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer C. Mosquito nets and insecticides target the vector; vaccination and antibiotics do not target mosquitoes; boiling water prevents waterborne diseases.
